note_data.title: Enzymes
keyPoints: **Enzymes** are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions without being used up
Enzymes are **proteins** with a specific three-dimensional shape determined by their amino acid sequence
The **active site** has a complementary shape to the substrate and forms an enzyme-substrate complex
Enzymes work by **lowering activation energy** required for a reaction to occur
**Temperature** affects enzyme activity - increasing temperature increases kinetic energy and collision frequency until optimum temperature
Above optimum temperature, enzymes become **denatured** - the active site changes shape permanently and cannot bind to substrate
**pH** affects enzyme activity - each enzyme has an optimum pH, extreme pH denatures enzymes
**Substrate concentration** affects rate of reaction until all active sites are occupied (saturation point)
Enzymes show **specificity** - each enzyme catalyses only one type of reaction due to the specific shape of its active site

definitions.definition: A biological catalyst that speeds up chemical reactions without being used up, made of protein
The part of an enzyme molecule with a complementary shape to the substrate where the substrate binds
The substance that an enzyme acts upon during a chemical reaction
When an enzyme's active site permanently changes shape due to high temperature or extreme pH, making it non-functional
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up or changed in the reaction

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of what happens step by step
Give similarities AND differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: Explain why enzyme activity decreases at high temperatures. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: At high temperatures, enzymes become denatured. The high temperature breaks the bonds holding the enzyme's shape together, causing the active site to change shape permanently. The substrate can no longer bind to the active site as they are no longer complementary in shape.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Substrate cannot bind to active site/no longer complementary

commonMistakes: Never say 'enzymes die' — always say 'enzymes become denatured'
Students often confuse optimum temperature with body temperature - optimum varies for different enzymes
Never say 'enzymes are used up' — enzymes are reusable and remain unchanged after reaction
Students forget that denaturation is permanent - don't say enzymes can be 'fixed' after denaturation
Don't say 'active site breaks' — say 'active site changes shape'
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially enzyme-temperature graphs
Always use the term 'complementary shape' when describing enzyme-substrate binding
Questions on digestive enzymes (amylase, pepsin, lipase) are very common
Draw enzyme-substrate complex diagrams clearly showing shape change
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Lock and key model showing enzyme with complementary-shaped active site, substrate binding to form enzyme-substrate complex, then products released with enzyme unchanged
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q2 (3 marks) — they asked students to explain the lock-and-key hypothesis for enzyme action, many lost marks for not mentioning complementary shapes
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice on enzyme specificity where students confused active site with substrate
M/J 2024 P2 Q4b (4 marks) — outline the role of enzymes in digestion, required specific examples of enzymes and their substrates
N/D 2023 P3 Q2 (6 marks) — practical investigation on effect of temperature on enzyme activity, testing graph interpretation skills
M/J 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — identifying organs that secrete digestive enzymes from a diagram

workedExample.question: Explain how the structure of an enzyme is related to its function. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: State that enzymes are proteins with a specific 3D shape
Step 2: Describe the active site as the region where substrate binds
Step 3: Explain complementary shapes between active site and substrate
Step 4: Link shape to specificity and catalytic function
workedExample.answer: Enzymes are proteins with a specific three-dimensional shape formed by folding of the polypeptide chain. The active site is a region of the enzyme with a specific shape that is complementary to the substrate molecule. Only substrates with the correct shape can bind to the active site, making enzymes specific for particular reactions. When the substrate binds to the active site, it forms an enzyme-substrate complex which lowers the activation energy needed for the reaction to occur.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for protein structure, 1 mark for active site description, 1 mark for complementary shapes/specificity, 1 mark for lowering activation energy

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the lock-and-key model with induced fit model' — always stick to lock-and-key unless specifically asked about induced fit
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students often wrote that enzymes provide energy for reactions' — remember enzymes lower activation energy, they don't provide energy
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates lost marks for vague statements like enzymes speed up reactions' — always specify that enzymes are biological catalysts that remain unchanged
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the enzyme-substrate complex diagram showing: (1) Enzyme with clearly labeled active site having specific shape, (2) Substrate molecule with complementary shape approaching active site, (3) Enzyme-substrate complex formation, (4) Products being released while enzyme remains unchanged. Include labels for enzyme, substrate, active site, enzyme-substrate complex, and products. Show that enzyme shape is unchanged after reaction.
note_data.quickRecap: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up reactions by lowering activation energy. They have specific active sites with shapes complementary to their substrates (lock-and-key hypothesis). Each enzyme is specific for one type of reaction due to the unique shape of its active site. Enzymes remain unchanged after the reaction and can be reused multiple times.
